content:
# Leviticus Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59v–60r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_005_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_005_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** "If a person sins by hearing a public call to testify — when someone adjures the community to come forward as a witness and the person knows something, either having seen or learned of it, yet fails to speak up — then that person bears the consequences of their sin.

**2** "Or if a person touches anything ceremonially unclean — whether the carcass of an unclean wild animal, or an unclean livestock animal, or an unclean crawling creature — even if they were unaware of it, they have become unclean and they are guilty.

**3** "Or if a person touches human uncleanness — any form of uncleanness by which a person becomes defiled — and they were unaware of it at the time, but then later realize it, they are guilty.

**4** "Or if a person thoughtlessly takes an oath to do something — whether it is to do harm or to do good, whatever kind of rash oath a person might swear — and they were unaware of it at the time, but then realize it, they are guilty in any of these cases.

**5** "When a person is guilty in any of these cases, they must confess the specific sin they have committed. **6** They shall bring to the LORD as their penalty for the sin they have committed a female animal from the flock — either a lamb or a goat — as a purification offering. The priest will make atonement for them for their sin.

**7** "If they cannot afford a sheep, they shall bring to the LORD, as their penalty for the sin they have committed, two doves or two young pigeons — one for a purification offering and the other for a burnt offering. **8** They shall bring them to the priest, who shall first offer the one designated for the purification offering. He shall wring its neck without completely severing it, **9** sprinkle some of the blood of the purification offering against the side of the altar, and drain out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar; it is a purification offering. **10** He shall offer the second bird as a burnt offering in the prescribed manner. In this way the priest will make atonement for them for the sin they have committed, and they will be forgiven.

**11** "If they cannot afford two doves or two young pigeons, they shall bring as their offering for the sin they committed a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a purification offering. They must not pour oil on it or put incense on it, because it is a purification offering. **12** They shall bring it to the priest, who shall take a handful of it as a token portion and burn it on the altar on top of the fire-offerings to the LORD; it is a purification offering. **13** In this way the priest will make atonement for them for whichever of these sins they have committed, and they will be forgiven. The remainder belongs to the priest, as with the grain offering."

**14** The LORD spoke to Moses: **15** "When a person commits a breach of sacred trust and sins unintentionally against any of the LORD's holy things, they shall bring to the LORD as their penalty a ram without defect from the flock, assessed at the proper value in silver shekels according to the sanctuary standard — it is a guilt offering. **16** They shall make full restitution for the holy thing they failed to honor, add a fifth of its value, and give it all to the priest. The priest will make atonement for them with the ram of the guilt offering, and they will be forgiven.

**17** "If a person sins and does any of the things the LORD has forbidden — even if they were not aware of it — they are still guilty and bear the weight of their wrongdoing. **18** They shall bring to the priest a ram without defect from the flock, valued at the proper amount — it is a guilt offering. The priest will make atonement for them for what they did in ignorance without realizing it, and they will be forgiven. **19** It is a guilt offering; they were genuinely liable before the LORD."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The confessional moment — ve-hitvaddah.** Verse 5 contains one of the most significant single words in the sacrificial system: *ve-hitvaddah* — "and they shall confess." The verb is *yadah* in its Hitpa'el form, the Hebrew reflexive stem, which means something like "they shall call out against themselves." The Hitpa'el of *yadah* is not passive acknowledgment; it is active, spoken, public self-disclosure of the specific wrong. The text does not say merely "feel remorse" or "be inwardly aware." It says: name it. Say what it was. This is the first explicit appearance of *hitvaddah* in the Torah, and it stands at the hinge between guilt and remedy. The animal alone does not move the machinery. The blood does not flow without the word. Confession in Leviticus is not optional emotional preparation; it is structurally integral to the whole transaction. You must name the sin to the priest — and the grammar suggests you name it to God — before the offering has any meaning. This anticipates the great confessional texts of the Hebrew Bible: the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16:21), David's *chatati la-YHWH* ("I have sinned against the LORD," 2 Samuel 12:13), and the entire penitential tradition of the Psalms.

**The four specific cases of chapter 5 — a pastoral taxonomy of sin.** Verses 1–4 identify four specific situations that require the purification offering of chapter 5. Each is worth examining individually, because together they map out a remarkable pastoral theology of guilt.

The first case (v. 1) is the failure to testify. A person *shama qol alah* — "hears the voice of an oath/adjuration." The community has formally called for witnesses. Someone knows something. They stay silent. This is a sin of omission, not commission — but it is a sin nonetheless. The person who could have spoken truthfully and did not has caused real harm through silence. Truth-telling in community has a structural obligation in Leviticus; you cannot hide behind private knowledge when the community needs your voice.

The second and third cases (vv. 2–3) are accidental contact with uncleanness — touching a carcass, or a person who is defiled. The striking feature is the phrase *ve-ne'elam mimmennu* — "and it was hidden from him," i.e., he didn't know. The guilt is real. The uncleanness is real. But the ignorance is morally relevant. The offering required here (a lamb, or birds, or flour) is less severe than the bull required for the congregation in chapter 4. Leviticus does not pretend that ignorance is culpable in the same way as defiance; it simply insists that even accidental defilement creates a problem that needs a remedy.

The fourth case (v. 4) is the rash oath — *le-vatte bisfatayim*, literally "to blurt out with the lips." The word *vatta* comes from a root meaning to speak rashly, to blurt, to say something without weighing it. The oath itself may have been to do something good or something harmful; what matters is that it was taken without due consideration. The act of swearing invokes God's name as guarantor and creates a moral obligation. Breaking that oath — or being unable to keep it — is not a trivial matter even when it was made thoughtlessly. This case sits at the intersection of speech, promise, and the weight of invoking the divine name.

**Asham — guilt, liability, and reparation.** The second half of the chapter (vv. 14–19) introduces the *asham*, which English Bibles typically render "guilt offering" but which is more precisely a "reparation offering" or even a "liability offering." The Hebrew root *'-sh-m* carries the sense of being in a state of indebtedness — not merely feeling guilty, but actually being legally or morally liable. You owe something. You have taken what was not yours, or failed to render what was required. The *asham* is the mechanism for settling that account.

What distinguishes the *asham* sharply from the *chattat* (purification offering) of chapters 4–5:1–13 is the restitution component. Not only must the guilty party bring an animal — a ram of assessed monetary value — but they must also make *full restitution* of the holy thing misappropriated *plus one-fifth* (Hebrew: *ve-et-chamishitho*, literally "and its fifth"). This surcharge of twenty percent is one of the Torah's earliest monetary penalties. It appears again in Leviticus 22:14 (eating sacred food accidentally) and in Numbers 5:7 (restitution to an injured party with no surviving relative). The one-fifth is not punitive in a harsh sense; it is restorative plus — you pay back what you took, plus something more, to signal that the relationship has been genuinely repaired and not merely reset to zero.

The word *ma'al* (v. 15), translated "breach of sacred trust," deserves its own attention. *Ma'al* is a strong term — it describes a kind of treachery or faithless act, most often in contexts of violating a sacred covenant or holy obligation. It is the word used when Achan secretly takes the devoted things from Jericho (Joshua 7:1), when Israel breaks faith by intermarrying with foreign peoples (Ezra 10:2), and in Ezekiel's repeated indictments of Israel's unfaithfulness. When *ma'al* is applied to accidental trespass against holy things in Leviticus 5, it shows how seriously the text takes even inadvertent violations of sacred space. The holy things belong to the LORD; to use them carelessly — even unknowingly — is a form of faithlessness. The word implies a breach in a personal relationship, not merely a procedural violation.

**The graduated offering system — theology from the bottom up.** Verses 7–13 contain one of the most striking pastoral provisions in all of Leviticus: a graduated offering scale that extends all the way down to a handful of flour. If you cannot afford a lamb, bring two birds. If you cannot afford two birds, bring flour. The word for this accommodation is *lo-tagia yado* — literally "his hand does not reach to," idiom for "cannot afford." Leviticus acknowledges poverty without shame and builds access to atonement into the structure of the law itself. No Israelite, however destitute, is excluded from the means of grace. The poorest person's flour offering is accepted and effective — the priest "will make atonement for them, and they will be forgiven," with no qualification, no second-tier status, no footnote about the diminished efficacy of grain versus blood.

There is, however, one mark of distinction for the poor person's offering: "They must not pour oil on it or put incense on it" (v. 11). This is not a stigma; it is a signal. The flour for the purification offering must be austere, plainly itself — unlike the grain offerings of Leviticus 2, which were enriched with oil and sometimes incense. The austerity marks its function: this is not a gift or a tribute or a festive offering. It is a purification — and the unadorned quality of the flour communicates the gravity and honesty of the act. You come with nothing but the minimum required, and the minimum required is accepted.

**The ram of assessed value — money enters the sanctuary.** Verse 15 introduces something new: the priest is to assess the value of the *asham* ram in silver shekels according to the sanctuary standard (*be-shiklekha kesef be-shekel ha-kodesh*). This is remarkable — a sacrificial animal is given a monetary value, not merely presented as a living creature. The assessment likely served two purposes: first, to ensure that the animal was of sufficient worth relative to the trespass committed, so that a trivial animal could not substitute for a serious breach; second, to create a standard by which the associated monetary restitution could be calculated. The sanctuary shekel was the official standard of weight kept in the tabernacle/temple precinct, ensuring that restitution payments were not devalued by using lighter commercial weights. The introduction of monetary assessment into the guilt offering is the first step toward the monetization of atonement that will culminate in the later temple economy — and, eventually, in the money-changers Jesus will confront in the temple courts.

**Verse 17–18 — guilt without awareness.** The final case of the chapter (vv. 17–18) is philosophically striking: "If a person sins and does any of the things the LORD has forbidden — even if they were not aware of it — they are still guilty." This is sometimes called the "floating guilt" or "objective guilt" principle. The person *does not know* what specific holy thing they have trespassed. They have a vague sense that something is wrong, perhaps a feeling of liability without a clear accounting of the cause. Leviticus takes this seriously: the subjective uncertainty does not eliminate the objective moral state. You are *ashem* — genuinely liable — even before you can name what you did. The ram of the guilt offering is brought, atonement is made, and forgiveness follows. But the text does not demand that you fully understand your sin before you can be forgiven for it. There is pastoral mercy here for the person who knows they have fallen short but cannot fully identify where.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations.** Vaticanus pages 93–94 (images pa_0089–0090) carry Leviticus 5 in the same careful uncial hand. Several LXX choices are illuminating.

At verse 1, the LXX renders *qol alah* as *φωνὴν ὁρκισμοῦ* (*phōnēn horkismou* — "the voice of an oath/adjuration"), preserving the legal character of the call to testify. The Greek *horkismos* carries the sense of a formal oath administered to another, which accurately captures the force of the Hebrew *alah* here — this is not merely a rumor or a suggestion but a formal adjuration by public authority.

At verse 5, *ve-hitvaddah* is rendered *ἐξαγορεύσει* (*exagoreusein* — "to confess, to declare openly"). The *exagoreuo* word family in Greek means to speak out, to disclose, to make known what was concealed — the *ex-* prefix carries a sense of "out" or "forth." This is an exact functional equivalent to the Hebrew Hitpa'el reflexive: you bring out what was inside, you speak forward what was hidden. The LXX thus captures the *public, spoken* quality of confessional self-disclosure that the Hebrew grammar encodes.

At verse 15, *ma'al* is rendered *ἀδικία* (*adikia* — "injustice, unrighteousness, wrongdoing") in some LXX traditions, though Vaticanus appears to use a form closer to *πλημμελεία* (*plēmmeleia* — "a false note, a misstep, an error that violates harmony"). *Plēmmeleia* is the LXX's characteristic translation for *asham* and related terms and is one of the more creative Greek choices in the entire LXX: it is a musical metaphor, literally "playing out of tune" or "striking the wrong note." Sin as dissonance — the breach of sacred trust as a false note in the cosmic music — is a distinctly Greek interpretive gloss on the Hebrew concept of objective liability. The image is different but the pastoral function is the same: something in the order of things has been disturbed, and it must be brought back into harmony.

At verse 15, the phrase *shekel ha-kodesh* ("sanctuary shekel") is rendered *σίκλους ἱεροὺς* (*siklous hierous* — "holy shekels"), preserving both the monetary and the sacred register of the assessment. The LXX does not attempt to convert the weight to a Greek equivalent; it transliterates *shekel* (*siklos*) and calls it holy.

At verse 16, the *chamishitho* surcharge — the one-fifth penalty — is rendered *τὸ πέμπτον αὐτοῦ* (*to pempton autou* — "its fifth"), precisely matching the Hebrew fraction. No interpretive expansion here; Vaticanus follows the MT exactly and expects the reader to understand the restitution-plus-surcharge principle from the bare fraction.

The most theologically significant LXX choice in the chapter is at verse 19: *ἀδικῶν ἠδίκησεν κυρίῳ* (*adikōn ēdikēsen kyriō* — "in wrongdoing he committed wrong against the LORD"). The double use of the *adikia* root (wrongdoing/wronged) in the Greek intensifies what the Hebrew phrase *asham asham la-YHWH* conveys through its double *asham*: the person is not merely guilty in a legal sense but has actively wronged God personally. The LXX makes the relational dimension explicit: the trespass is an injustice committed *against the LORD* — not merely a ritual irregularity, not merely a procedural failure, but a wrong done to a person. This relational framing of the guilt offering — the *asham* as an offense against a relationship, not merely a violation of a rule — is the interpretive key to understanding why the guilt offering requires both the animal and the restitution. You must repair not just the account but the relationship.

*— PNEUMA*
